Home › Redshift in Astronomy (\(z\))

Redshift in Astronomy (\(z\))

Redshift (\( z \)) is the phenomenon where the light from an object is shifted toward longer (redder) wavelengths. In university-level astrophysics, redshift is the primary observable for measuring the kinematics of the universe. It is a composite effect, arising from the Doppler Effect (motion through space), Gravitational Redshift (curvature of space), and most importantly, Cosmological Redshift (the expansion of space itself).

1. The Dimensionless Measure of Shift

Redshift is defined as the fractional change in the wavelength of a spectral line: \[ z = \frac{\lambda_{obs} - \lambda_{rest}}{\lambda_{intrinsic}} \] Because redshift is a ratio, it is a dimensionless number. Astronomers measure \( z \) by identifying the unique "Fingerprints" of atoms (like the H-alpha line or calcium H&K lines) in a galaxy's spectrum and comparing their observed positions to laboratory standards, providing an absolute measure of the 's stretching since the light was emitted.

2. Cosmological Redshift and a(t)

Unlike the Doppler effect, which is caused by objects "moving" away, cosmological redshift is caused by the Metric Expansion of space. As space stretches, the wavelength of a photon traveling through that space is stretched by the exact same factor. This leads to the fundamental relationship between redshift and the Scale Factor: \[ 1+z = \frac{1}{a(t_{emit})} \] This equation proves that redshift is a "Time Machine": a higher redshift means we are looking further back into the History of the Universe.

3. Hubble's Law and the Velocity-Distance Link

At low redshifts (\( z \ll 1 \)), the redshift is approximately proportional to the recessional velocity: \( v \approx cz \). When combined with Hubble's Law (\( v = H_0 d \)), this provides the final "Rung" of the Cosmic Distance Ladder. By measuring the redshift of a galaxy, astronomers can estimate its distance, allowing for the construction of 3D maps of the "Cosmic Web" and the detection of large-scale Dynamics.

4. Gravitational Redshift (Einstein Effect)

Redshift can also be caused by gravity. According to General Relativity, light emitted from a deep potential well (like the surface of a Neutron Star or near a Black Hole) must lose energy to climb out of the curved spacetime. This results in a shift toward redder wavelengths given by \( z \approx GM/rc^2 \). This "Einstein Effect" is a precision test of the Equivalence Principle and is used to measure the mass-to-radius ratio of compact objects.

5. Peculiar Velocity and Redshift Space Distortions

A galaxy's total redshift is \( z_{tot} = z_{cosmo} + z_{doppler} \). The Doppler part, caused by the galaxy's local motion within its cluster, is called its Peculiar Velocity. These velocities create "Redshift Space Distortions"—apparent elongation or squashing of galaxy clusters in 3D maps (Fingers of God). Analyzing these distortions allows physicists to measure the Growth Rate of structure and the properties of Dark Matter.

6. Redshift as a Cosmological Clock

Redshift defines the eras of the universe.

  • z = 0: Today.
  • z \approx 1,100: The era of Recombination and the CMB.
  • z \approx 20: The "Cosmic Dawn" when the first stars formed.
The high-redshift universe is the primary target for the James Webb Space Telescope, which uses infrared sensors to see light that was emitted as ultraviolet but has been redshifted by a factor of 10 or more, linking the subatomic physics of the Big Bang to the visible stars of today.

Key Theoretical Identities

Interact with identities to explore their semantic depth.

  • Wavelength Stretch [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ z = \frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Wavelength Stretch: The mathematical definition of redshift as the ratio of changed to original light color.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Geometric Expansion [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ 1 + z = \frac{1}{a(t)} \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Geometric Expansion: The rule that relates the universe's size to the amount of light stretching.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Hubble Projection [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ v = cz \quad (z \ll 1) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Hubble Projection: How redshift is used to estimate how fast a galaxy is "flying" away.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Relativistic Reddening [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ z_{grav} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2GM/rc^2}} - 1 \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Relativistic Reddening: Why light loses energy when trying to escape from a heavy mass.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.
